name: Measurability & in-session completability
scenario: Are the acceptance criteria measurable and verifiable, and can an agent complete every criterion within one working session (no real-user validation, no long waits, no host-live ops that can't be simulated with provided auth)? Flag criteria that are unmeasurable ('works well'), or that require out-of-session validation.

name: User/problem present
scenario: Does the plan state WHO the work is for and WHAT problem it solves (a user, a caller, a failure being fixed)? A plan that is pure mechanism with no stated problem/beneficiary is weaker. Light content check; for low-level tasks an implied technical consumer counts.

name: Criteria<->description coherence + terminology + duplicates
scenario: Does every acceptance criterion map to something described in the plan body, and vice-versa (no orphan criteria, no described work with no criterion)? Is terminology used consistently (the same concept named the same way throughout)? Are there duplicate or near-duplicate requirements?

name: Ambiguity / executable-without-clarification
scenario: Could an executing agent act on this plan WITHOUT stopping to ask a clarifying question? Flag ambiguity, undercooked placeholders (spike/research/TBD/'figure out'), and explicit decision-deferral ('choose an appropriate X', 'decide later') that pushes a real design decision onto the executor.

name: Intent fidelity
scenario: Does the body of the plan faithfully serve the stated title/goal, with no scope drift (doing more or less than the title promises) and no contradiction between the headline intent and the detailed steps?

name: Testing-plan completeness
scenario: Does the plan describe how the change will be tested? Look for behavioral/contractual coverage, failure-path and boundary cases, change-detector avoidance, and RED-before-GREEN ordering where applicable. Note: structural greps / command-output assertions ARE a legitimate cross-language test pattern — do not flag them as weak.

name: Verification/termination + end-state reachability
scenario: Is there a clear way to verify the work is done and to know when to stop? Does the UNION of the described steps actually reach the stated end-state in the acceptance criteria (no gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like')?

name: Decomposition judgment
scenario: Is the ticket appropriately sized? Flag if it is too big for one unit of work and should be split into children, or split along a horizontal-layer seam where a vertical slice would be safer. For an epic/parent: is decomposition into children present and sensible? For a leaf: is it small enough to execute coherently in one session?

name: Assumption/premise verification
scenario: Does the plan rest on unverified assertions about the codebase or environment ('the X module already does Y', 'there is a helper that...')? Flag claims that an executor would need to verify against the actual code before relying on them. (You are reviewing plan text only; where a claim can only be confirmed against the live codebase you lack, mark AMBIGUOUS and name the assumption rather than asserting it is wrong.)

name: Edit-set / scope accuracy
scenario: Are the files/symbols the plan intends to touch named and plausible, and is the blast radius acknowledged? Flag missing consumers/callers that a change of this kind would require updating, or edit targets that seem hallucinated/vague. High blast-radius alone is NOT a failure if acknowledged. (Plan-text only; mark AMBIGUOUS where confirmation needs the live codebase.)

name: Anti-slop / over-engineering
scenario: Does the plan reinvent something that likely already exists (NIH), proliferate configuration surface, or introduce gratuitous abstraction (a new layer/interface used at only one call-site — Rule-of-Three)? Flag over-engineering and not-invented-here. (Plan-text only; mark AMBIGUOUS where confirmation needs the live codebase.)

name: Cross-section coherence
scenario: Are there contradictions BETWEEN sections of the plan — e.g. the testing strategy contradicts the decomposition, the sequencing contradicts the declared dependencies, the context contradicts the success criteria? Single structured pass for internal contradictions across the whole plan.
